title: MathLesson Training Pack 01 — Basic Feature Tour v1.1
description: A practical first training lesson for learning the basic MathLesson authoring workflow and testing common content blocks.

sections.label: Start
sections.title: What this lesson tests
sections.content:
MathLesson Basic Feature Tour

This lesson is a training file for the author. It is designed to be imported, edited, exported as JSON, re-imported, and exported as a student lesson.

What this lesson tests
This file tests prose, headings, inline LaTeX, display LaTeX, callouts, grids, tables, SVG, inline exercise widgets, formal quiz items, and basic export layout controls.
Author task
After importing this lesson, find the Lesson Editor, add one sentence to this section, then export the active lesson as JSON and re-import it.

Inline math test: if $f(x)=x^2+2x+1$, then $f(x)=(x+1)^2$.

Display math test
$$x^2+2x+1=(x+1)^2$$

sections.label: Blocks
sections.title: Prose, math, and callouts
sections.content:
Prose, Math, and Callout Blocks

A good lesson should not be only formulas. It should combine explanation, named ideas, examples, checkpoints, and short exercises.

Definition
A feature block is a visible authoring unit that should survive preview, JSON export, student export, and print/export planning.
Rule of thumb
One section should usually have one main instructional purpose. If a section has many graphs, many formulas, and many tables, split it.
Identity
$$a^2-b^2=(a-b)(a+b)$$
Example
For $a=x$ and $b=3$, the identity becomes $x^2-9=(x-3)(x+3)$.
Proof block
Expand the product: $(a-b)(a+b)=a^2+ab-ab-b^2=a^2-b^2$. This block is marked with avoid-break so it should stay together when possible.
Mini exercise
Factor $x^2-16$ before looking at the quiz.

sections.label: Layout
sections.title: Grids, tables, and SVG
sections.content:
Grids, Tables, and SVG

This section tests common layout elements that authors will use before reaching advanced renderers.

Explain

Use paragraphs and callouts to explain the goal.

Compute

Use display math, tables, and examples.

$$2^3=8$$

Check

Use exercises and quiz questions to test understanding.

BlockUseExport note
CalloutNamed idea or warningUsually keep together
GridCompare related ideasMay need a slide break
TableStructured informationWide tables may need landscape

sections.label: Practice
sections.title: Inline exercise widgets
sections.content:
Inline Exercise Widgets

Inline exercises are local checks inside a lesson section. Formal quiz questions live in the Quiz panel and are exported as a learner assessment.

Simplify $\frac{x^2+5x+4}{x+1}$.
Compute $2^3$.
UX observation prompt
While testing this lesson, note how long it takes to find where quizzes are edited versus where inline exercises are inserted.

sections.label: Export
sections.title: Basic export controls
sections.content:
Basic Export Controls

This section gives simple layout-control examples. It should be checked in Print/PDF preview and Reveal export planning.

Keep together
This theorem-like block should avoid splitting across a printed page when possible.
$$a(b+c)=ab+ac$$

The horizontal rule above is an explicit Reveal slide break.

The horizontal rule above is an explicit Print/PDF page break.

This paragraph is inside an allow-break wrapper. It is allowed to split if necessary. Use this only for ordinary prose, not for a theorem, table, graph, or diagram that should remain visually intact.

quiz.stem: Where should formal assessment questions be tested?
options: In the Quiz panel and student export
quiz.feedback: Formal assessment belongs in the quiz workflow and should be tested in student export.

quiz.stem: Inline exercise widgets and formal quiz items serve exactly the same purpose.
quiz.feedback: False. Inline exercises are local practice; quiz items are formal assessment.
The expression is a perfect square.

quiz.stem: Write one sentence identifying the hardest action to find in the app after importing this lesson.
quiz.feedback: Use this response to inform the later UX/UI redesign.
